midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or a board wipe ready. Hold back if the opponent has significant interaction or if you're behind on board. Use your Initiative to answer threats or stabilize rather than just going wide.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for the opponent's graveyard (especially against Dredge/Oath), their mana base (to predict if they can hit 4+ mana for Lorien or key spells), and their interaction count. Key tells include holding up blue mana (counterspell/spot removal) or black mana (Lurrus/Graveyard hate).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large threats cast from the top of your library, or combo pieces that win immediately. Lorien Revealed itself can be a win condition if you can cast it multiple times or protect it while casting game-ending spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear board advantage, the opponent has no interaction, or you've established a reliable Initiative engine. Also commit when you can cast a high-impact spell from the top that swings the board.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're consistently losing early games to aggro, have no way to find Lorien Revealed, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., Oath of Druids with multiple creatures, or Dredge with infinite life). Also concede if you're dead to the deck's primary win conditions and have no answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost threats to force counters, then cast higher-value spells from the top. Utilize instant-speed interaction or protection spells. Don't overcommit to a single spell if you can split your threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal to kill their ramp creatures. If they get ahead, stabilize with board wipes and out-value them with Initiative recursion.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and spot removal. Use your Initiative to cast removal or counterspells from the top. Don't overextend into board wipes; stabilize first, then close out.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats that are hard to remove. Use your Initiative to out-value their card draw. Force them to use interaction on your threats, then swing with the top of your library.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize protecting your Lorien Revealed and Initiative creatures. Use targeted removal to kill their key pieces. Sideboard in additional counterspells and graveyard hate if needed. Out-value them by casting more spells from the top.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against slower decks, bring in more late-game threats and card advantage. Against faster decks, prioritize early interaction and protection. Always ensure you have enough lands and fixing to consistently hit your mana requirements. Review your sideboard plan before each game and adapt based on the opponent's first-game performance.
notes: Initiative recursion is powerful but vulnerable to board wipes and targeted removal. Always protect Lorien Revealed. Be mindful of your library size; running out of cards can limit your Initiative value. Practice sequencing your turns to maximize spell-casting from the top. Common mistake: overcommitting to casting low-value spells from the top instead of saving mana for protection or interaction.

philosophy: Assemble a high-mana artifact engine or win condition in a single turn by leveraging cheap cantrips, mana acceleration, and Beseech the Mirror to sacrifice creatures for free artifacts. Wins against combo and control decks by out-resolving or out-valueing them, while struggling against fast aggro and graveyard strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Dark Ritual or Flow State, Beseech the Mirror, and a creature to sacrifice (or a way to generate one). Mulligan on all lands, all spells without acceleration, or hands missing Beseech with no way to cast it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip (e.g., Delve spell, Brainstorm, or similar) to dig for acceleration/Beseech. Turn 2: Cast Dark Ritual/Flow State, play a land, cast Beseech the Mirror, sacrifice a creature to search for an artifact, cast it for free. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast acceleration on time without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Esper Lurrus Control: +1 disruption (Thoughtseize, Duress), +1 hatebear (Thousand-Year Elixir), -2 draw spells. vs Jewel Shops: +1 artifact hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), +1 early interaction, -2 acceleration. vs Dredge: +1 graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), +1 early interaction, -2 Beseech/late game. vs Oath of Druids: +1 disruption, +1 hatebear, -2 draw spells. vs Mono-White Initiative: +1 spot removal, +1 early interaction, -2 acceleration.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping hand size high with cantrips; don't overcommit to casting Beseech if you lack a creature to sacrifice or a clear win condition. Manage graveyard carefully if facing Dredge. Use Dark Ritual/Flow State only when you can cast Beseech and have a target.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting Beseech if the opponent has sweepers or counterspells. Commit when you have a clear path to lethal or a lock piece. Don't overextend with creatures if you can win via artifacts.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up mana), graveyard strategies (track dredge/putrefy), and control win conditions (Lurrus, planeswalkers). Note when opponents tap out for sweepers or hatebears.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Beseech the Mirror into free artifacts (e.g., Thopter Foundry + Sword, or a combo like Phyrexian Revoker + Thousand-Year Elixir, or just artifact value leading to lethal). Sometimes finding a direct win con like a free-to-cast threat or combo piece.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have a complete mana base, a sacrifice outlet, Beseech, and a clear win condition in your library/hand. Go all-in if the opponent has no interaction left or if you're ahead in the race.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a fast combo/aggro and have no early interaction or win condition, or if you're locked out by graveyard hate/sweepers with no way to recover. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no acceleration or Beseech.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig around counters, cast Beseech in response to counterspells if possible, or use cheap acceleration to cast Beseech when they're tapped out. Sideboard in protection or alternative win cons.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with your combo, or use disruption (Thoughtseize, Duress) to hit their key ramp spells. Don't let them stabilize; pressure early.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (discard, spot removal, or early Beseech if you can find a blocker). Don't overcommit to the combo if you're losing the board. Sideboard in hate and early threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with artifact synergy, use cantrips to dig past their removal, and pressure with early artifacts. If they have sweepers, wait for a turn they can't respond, or use protection.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by drawing more cards, using disruption to hit their Beseech/acceleration, and prioritizing artifact value. Sideboard in extra disruption and value engines.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo to a more resilient game plan. Against control/combo, bring in disruption and hatebears to protect your engine. Against aggro/graveyard, prioritize early interaction and hate. Adjust land counts if needed, and ensure you have a mix of acceleration, cantrips, and win conditions for the post-board meta.
notes: Common mistakes: Casting Beseech without a creature to sacrifice, overcommitting to the combo against sweepers, ignoring graveyard strategies, and not tracking opponent's mana. Unique interactions: Flow State can be cast for {0} if you control an artifact, enabling extra turns or massive value. Beseech's sacrifice target can be any creature you control, including tokens or artifacts with the creature type (if applicable). Always have a backup plan if your primary win con is countered or removed.

philosophy: A control-combo hybrid that stabilizes early with efficient removal and card draw, then leverages Lurrus of the Dream-Den to repeatedly cast Goblin Welder and Goblin Engineer, assembling an infinite draw engine to find win conditions or overwhelm opponents. It excels against control and combo by out-grinding them, while relying on speed and protection to close out games against aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals/shocklands or basics that hit WUR), a Lurrus or early threat (Goblin Guide/Monastery Swiftspear), and at least one interaction (Inventor's Inspiration, Lightning Strike, or Spell Pierce). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or 2+ lands with only removal and no Lurrus/threats. Keep hands that can cast Lurrus on turn 2 or 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-drop (Goblin Guide, Monastery Swiftspear, or Spell Pierce/Inventor's Inspiration). Turn 2: Cast Lurrus if possible, or play a second land and hold for interaction. Against aggro, prioritize early threats and spot removal. Against control/combo, protect Lurrus and look for early interaction to enable turn 3-4 combo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in Lightning Strike, Spell Pierce, Surgical Extraction, and additional spot removal. vs Control/Combo: Bring in Inventor's Inspiration, Lightning Strike, Surgical Extraction, and counterspells (Negate/Spell Pierce). vs Mirror: Bring in counterspells, extra removal, and protection (Lightning Greaves, Swiftfoot Boots).

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Lurrus as your primary engine; avoid casting Welder/Engineer unless you can safely assemble the combo or have a clear win path. Manage the graveyard with Surgical Extraction or discard to prevent opponent recursion. Use Inventor's Inspiration to cheat on mana for combo pieces or Lurrus.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction or a clear win condition. Hold back Lurrus/Welder/Engineer if the board is unstable or lacks protection. Use removal efficiently to clear key threats (Oath of Druids, Initiative commander, Dredge creatures).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace), counterspells (Spell Pierce, Negate), and board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods). Track opponent's hand size and graveyard contents. Note if they're playing Oath of Druids (early mana dorks) or Dredge (Bitter Ordeal, Golgari Grave-Troll).

endgame_tactics.finishers: The Goblin Engineer + Goblin Welder + Lightning Greaves infinite draw combo, followed by casting Vault of the Archangel for life gain and card advantage, or using infinite resources to cast a game-winning spell. Alternatively, win with Monastery Swiftspear or Goblin Guide if the combo is disrupted.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection (Greaves/Boots), a clear path to assemble the combo, and no immediate board wipes or graveyard hate in play. Commit to the combo if you can resolve the first Engineer and have a way to protect it.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if facing a hard lock (Rest in Peace + Leyline, or Oath of Druids with no removal), if down to one card with no interaction, or if the opponent has stabilized with a board wipe and you lack a win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Inventor's Inspiration to cast combo pieces for free, or split the combo across multiple turns. Use Lightning Greaves to protect key creatures from targeted counterspells. Cast Goblin Engineer in response to counterspells when possible.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction (Spell Pierce, Lightning Strike) to disrupt mana dorks. Use Surgical Extraction to remove key ramp pieces from the graveyard. If they outpace you, commit to the combo early or win with early threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early threats and spot removal. Use Lightning Strike to kill 1-2 damage creatures. Hold back Lurrus until stabilized. Use Vault of the Archangel for life gain if needed.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with card draw and Lurrus recursion. Use Inventor's Inspiration to cheat on mana for combo pieces. Pressure with early threats to force interaction. If locked down, use Surgical Extraction to remove graveyard hate or key permanents.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize counterspells and removal. Use Lightning Greaves to protect combo pieces. Track Lurrus on the stack. Win by assembling the combo first or out-resolving their Lurrus plays.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor interaction to the metagame. Against control/combo, increase counterspells and graveyard hate. Against aggro, add spot removal and early threats. Ensure your mana base supports the combo (duals, shocklands, fetches if legal). Maintain a balance between control elements and combo pieces.
notes: The infinite combo requires precise sequencing: cast Goblin Engineer, attach Lightning Greaves, tap Engineer to sacrifice a 1/1 token (or any creature), draw 2, Engineer dies, Goblin Welder finds another Engineer, repeat. Use Inventor's Inspiration to cast Engineer for free. Be aware of graveyard hate and counterspells. Lurrus is your win condition enabler, so protect it at all costs. Avoid overcommitting to the combo without protection.

philosophy: Fastbond Lands is a high-variance ramp and combo archetype that uses Fastbond to draw cards by tapping lands, supported by Crucible of Worlds to exile and replay those lands, and Kaheera to protect your engine. It aims to out-value control and combo decks by establishing an unbreakable land base, then closing out games with overwhelming mana or combo finishers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with Fastbond, Crucible of Worlds, or Kaheera, the Orphanguard. Otherwise, keep 3+ lands plus at least one land-fetching spell (e.g., Rampant Growth, Farseek, Kodama’s Reach) or a protection spell. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, or multiple lands with no ramp/protection if you lack a signature card.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then cast a land-fetching spell or Kaheera if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Fastbond, tap it with a fetch land to draw 2 cards, then use Crucible of Worlds to exile the fetched land and replay it. Prioritize establishing 3-4 untapped lands by turn 3 to safely tap Fastbond repeatedly.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Control/Initiative: Bring in protection (e.g., Swords to Plowshares, Wrath of God, or land-specific protection), counters, or combo finishers. vs Jewel Shops/Dredge: Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) and artifact/enchantment removal. vs Oath of Druids: Bring in targeted removal, counters, or faster combo pieces to race. vs Mirror: Bring in additional protection, card draw, and land destruction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Crucible to exile lands from the graveyard to prevent opponent graveyard strategies and maintain your land count. Never tap Fastbond if you’re below 3 untapped lands unless you can safely replay the tapped land. Prioritize land-fetching over non-land ramp to keep your Fastbond engine fueled.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Kaheera early to protect Fastbond and fetch lands. Hold back on overcommitting to the board if you haven’t secured protection or a win condition. Use landfall triggers and card draw to maintain hand size and mana advantage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for targeted removal on Fastbond, land destruction (e.g., Blightsteel Colossus, Carrion Feeder), and graveyard hate. Note if opponents are playing discard or hand disruption, as your combo relies on card selection and consistent land drops.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Win with Emrakul, the Aeons Torn (to clear board and draw cards), Utopia Sprawl + massive mana, or infinite mana combos (e.g., Fastbond + fetch lands + landfall triggers). Alternatively, cast high-impact spells like Genesis Ultimatum or Dominate.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Fastbond protected, 5+ untapped lands, and a clear path to a win condition. Commit to a combo if you have the necessary pieces and protection against removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if Fastbond is permanently removed, you lack protection or recursion, and the opponent has a resilient board or hand disruption. Also concede if you’re on the draw with a dead hand and no way to stabilize against control or combo.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use protection spells, cast Fastbond/Kaheera in response to counters, or go off with multiple spells in a single turn. Utilize landfall triggers and instant-speed ramp to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with land-fetching spells and Fastbond card draw. Protect your lands and combo off before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early protection and board wipes. Use Kaheera to protect key lands, then stabilize with removal or a quick win condition. Don’t overextend into combat.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with landfall triggers and card draw. Force them to use resources on your lands, then combo off or win with a high-impact spell. Use protection to bypass counterspells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by protecting your Fastbond better, drawing more cards, and going off first. Use land destruction to disrupt their engine, and prioritize card advantage to out-resource them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your 10-card slot to the local meta. Against control-heavy formats, prioritize protection, counters, and combo finishers. Against graveyard or artifact-heavy decks, bring in hate cards and removal. In mirror matches, add more land destruction, protection, and card draw. Always keep 1-2 flexible answers to handle unexpected threats.
notes: Fastbond Lands is highly vulnerable to land destruction and targeted removal—never assume your engine is safe. Crucible of Worlds is your insurance policy; always exile lands you don’t need to keep your library from running out. Kaheera’s protection is single-target, so prioritize protecting Fastbond over other lands. Avoid tapping Fastbond with non-fetch lands if you can help it, as it doesn’t help your Crucible recursion. Watch for opponent’s graveyard strategies, as they can disrupt your engine.

philosophy: This deck aims to disrupt the opponent’s early game with cheap, efficient interaction (Flusterstorm, Psychic Frog, Collector Ouphe), stall until you can stabilize, and out-value them into the late game with efficient threats and card advantage. It excels against combo and control decks by neutralizing their win conditions, while relying on resource efficiency and sequencing to grind out midrange and mirror matches.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetch/shock or duals for U/B/G), at least one 1-mana interaction (Flusterstorm, Psychic Frog, or Collector Ouphe), and a curve of 1-2-3 spells. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, zero interaction, or multiple 3+ mana spells without early support or land acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Psychic Frog or Collector Ouphe if mana allows. Turn 2: Curve into a 2-drop (e.g., Thoughtseize, Go Blank, or a 2-drop threat). Sequence lands to ensure you can produce U for counters, B for discard, and G for ramp/threats. Hold up interaction for turn 1/2 threats or key combo pieces, and use Psychic Frog’s tap ability to dig for answers when your hand is light.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Initiative, Jewel Shops): Bring in artifact hate, additional discard, and early removal. vs Control (Esper Lurrus): Bring in card draw, late-game threats, and counters to protect your own spells. vs Combo (Dredge, Oath): Bring in graveyard hate, artifact hate, and specific disruption. vs Mirror: Bring in more interaction and card advantage to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve interaction for high-impact threats; avoid blowing counters on non-critical spells. Use Psychic Frog’s draw effect to dig for answers when your hand is empty. Manage your graveyard carefully against Dredge/Oath, and avoid unnecessary triggers that could fuel opponent strategies or reveal your hand to discard.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction to protect them or clear the way. Hold back if the opponent has a known sweep or heavy removal. Trade efficiently, prioritizing trades that leave you with card advantage or board presence, and avoid overextending into known sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), graveyard strategies (Dredge, Oath), and artifact engines (Jewel Shops, Initiative). Note when they tap out for big spells or when they start drawing multiple cards. Track their remaining interaction to time your own plays and identify when they are vulnerable to pressure.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Late-game threats like Tarmogoyf, Scavenging Ooze, or midrange creatures backed by card draw. Win by out-resourcing and applying pressure when interaction is exhausted, forcing the opponent to use remaining resources inefficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When the opponent has no interaction left, or when you have card advantage and can trade up. Go all-in if you can close the game before they can stabilize or find a sweep, especially if you have a proven win condition in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you’re down to one life, have no interaction, and the opponent has a proven win condition (like a resolved sweep + finisher or graveyard combo) with no way to disrupt it. Also concede if your hand is completely dead against a known lock or if you’re out of resources in a control mirror.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Psychic Frog’s draw to dig through, cast spells in response to their counters, or use uncounterable threats. Sequence around known counters and save interaction for their key spells. Force them to use counters on low-impact spells to open up your hand.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction to stop mana dorks/land drops. Collector Ouphe shuts down non-artifact ramp. Discard their ramp spells and pressure them before they can cast big threats. Don’t let them stabilize; trade efficiently and punish missed blocks.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early interaction to stall, discard key threats, and stabilize with efficient removal. Don’t overcommit to board stalls; transition to threats once stabilized. Use discard to disrupt their curve and force them to play around your interaction, slowing their aggression.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with efficient threats, force them to use resources, and out-value them with card draw. Don’t let them stabilize with Lurrus or sweepers. Trade efficiently and punish missed blocks. Force them to make the first mistake by applying consistent pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war. Out-value them with better sequencing, card draw, and sideboard cards. Prioritize keeping interaction for their key threats and force them to make the first mistake. Track their hand and graveyard to anticipate their plays.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Bring in hate cards for specific archetypes, and prioritize cards that synergize with your interaction shell. In control mirrors, prioritize card advantage and late-game threats. Against aggro, bring in more early interaction and removal. Always track what the opponent has left in their deck and adjust your play pattern accordingly, favoring conservative plays against unknown decks.
notes: Collector Ouphe is technically a white card, so ensure your mana base supports W if it’s in the deck, or note that the archetype may actually be U/B/W (Esper) but the prompt specifies U/B/G. Psychic Frog and Flusterstorm are blue, so U is primary. B provides discard/graveyard hate. G provides ramp/efficient threats. Avoid overcommitting to the board without interaction, as tempo decks die to sweepers. Always prioritize card advantage in the late game, and use Psychic Frog’s draw effect strategically to dig for answers rather than wasting it on non-critical spells.

philosophy: Prison Shops aims to deploy a dense web of mana-restricting and spell-locking artifacts early, neutralizing combo and control strategies before they can execute their game plans. By leveraging cheap mana acceleration to cast high-cost lock pieces, it transitions into a dominant midgame where Golos, Tireless Pilgrim, and other shop creatures generate enough mana to cast a decisive win condition or attack unblocked.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, Golos or Tireless Pilgrim, and at least one lock piece (Lodestone Golem, Sphere of Resistance). Keep hands with acceleration + a lock piece, or Golos + Pilgrim + 2 lands. Mulligan hands with only lock pieces and no mana sources, multiple lands with no early plays, or hands lacking a way to cast turn-1/2 threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a mana artifact or Golos/Pilgrim if mana allows. If you have acceleration, cast Lodestone Golem or Sphere of Resistance. Turn 2: Use Golos/Pilgrim to generate mana, cast additional lock pieces, or start setting up the win condition. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/playing lands that tap for blue or colorless, ensuring you can cast blue spells and artifacts.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro: Bring in discard, counters, and additional early lock pieces. Against Control (Esper Lurrus): Bring in graveyard hate, additional lock pieces, and counterspells. Against Combo (Dredge, Oath, Initiative): Bring in specific hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of Punishment, or additional lock pieces). Against Mirror: Bring in counterspells, additional lock pieces, and discard.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is king. Always prioritize casting lock pieces over drawing cards. Use Golos/Pilgrim to generate mana for win conditions, not just more locks. Manage hand by keeping answers for key threats. Graveyard is usually not a primary focus unless facing Dredge.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when the board is locked down. Hold back Golos/Pilgrim if you need them for mana to cast a win condition or counter a disruption. Prioritize locking the opponent's ability to cast spells or play lands over attacking.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard recursion (Dredge), mana acceleration (Oath), or artifact destruction (Initiative/Control). Key tells: Opponent playing multiple lands, casting cheap creatures, or targeting your artifacts.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Golos + Tireless Pilgrim generating massive mana to cast Emrakul, Aeons Torn, Ugin, or a combo finisher. Alternatively, attacking with a massive threat or using a combo like Urza's Saga.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have locked the opponent out of relevant interactions and have enough mana to cast a win condition or attack for lethal. Also commit when the opponent has no removal for your lock pieces.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: When you have no lock pieces, no mana acceleration, and the opponent has established a board or graveyard strategy you can't interact with. Also when you're out of resources and facing a combo that can win in one turn.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap lock pieces that don't need to be countered, or cast them in response to counterspells. Use counterspells yourself if in the deck. Rely on the fact that lock pieces are often cheap or free with acceleration.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Lock down their mana sources with Sphere of Resistance, Lodestone Golem, and additional hatebears. Use discard or counters to stop their key ramp pieces.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early lock pieces to stop their creatures. Use counterspells or discard to remove threats. Golos/Pilgrim can generate mana for removal or a big blocker.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with lock pieces to force them to use resources on interaction. Use Golos/Pilgrim to cast a win condition they can't answer. Outlast them by locking their graveyard and hand.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize locking their mana and hand. Use counterspells and discard to disrupt their setup. Golos/Pilgrim sequencing is key to out-generating their mana.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your lock pieces to the specific metagame. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and additional lock pieces. Against control, bring in counterspells and discard. Against aggro, bring in removal and early interaction. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't dilute your mana base or lock consistency.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting Golos/Pilgrim too early without a win condition, failing to lock the opponent's mana, or ignoring graveyard strategies. Unique interactions: Golos + Pilgrim can generate massive mana if you have enough artifacts. Sphere of Resistance + Lodestone Golem can make spells uncastable. Watch out for artifact destruction and graveyard recursion.

philosophy: A WU control deck that stabilizes early with efficient removal and counterspells, then transitions to a late-game grind using Thing in the Ice as a resilient threat and card advantage engine, supported by Lurrus of the Dream-Den to recur cheap permanents and maintain resource parity. It aims to outvalue midrange and combo decks while trading efficiently against control mirrors.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Swords to Plowshares or Dovin's Veto, and a turn-2 play. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands with no plays, or hands lacking early interaction against aggro/combo. Prioritize hands that can stabilize by turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, pass. Turn 2: Play second land, cast a 2-mana interaction (Dovin's Veto, Path to Exile, or Fog) or develop mana. If facing aggro, prioritize Swords to Plowshares or Fog on turn 2 if possible. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana availability for WU spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Dredge, Oath, MWI): +2 Fog, +2 Swords to Plowshares, -2 Dovin's Veto, -1 Thing in the Ice. Vs Control/Combo (Esper Lurrus, Jewel Shops): +2 Dovin's Veto, +2 Swords to Plowshares, -2 Fog. Adjust based on specific opponent tendencies.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 4-5. Don't overcommit to board presence early. Use Lurrus to replay cheap permanents from the graveyard to maintain hand size and board control. Track graveyard contents to avoid dead draws.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting Thing in the Ice until you have at least one removal spell or counter in hand, or until you can protect it. Prioritize trading cheap permanents for opponent's threats. Don't overextend into Jewel Shops or Oath.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard accumulation (Dredge), artifact acceleration (Jewel Shops), and Oath targets. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or combo pieces. Track if they've answered your Lurrus or Thing.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Thing in the Ice is the primary win condition. Use Lurrus to recur Swords/Path to clear blockers or replay Fog to reset the board. Card advantage from Thing's death trigger or Lurrus recursion closes games.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage locked, opponent has no removal/counter backup, or you can safely cast Thing and follow up with protection/recursion.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards, opponent has established an unanswerable win condition (e.g., Oath on board, Jewel Shops with infinite mana), or you're out of interaction against a resilient threat.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Swords to Plowshares or Path to Exile on their counterspells if they're creatures, or play around them by holding up interaction. Use Lurrus to recur cheap spells to pressure through. Consider Thing in the Ice as a threat that forces them to use multiple answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early removal (Swords, Path) on ramp targets. Use Fog or Dovin's Veto to stall. Don't let them get ahead; trade efficiently.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Swords to Plowshares, Path to Exile, and Fog. Mulligan aggressively for early interaction. Use Lurrus to replay removal to handle multiple threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Win the card advantage war. Use Thing in the Ice to force trades and draw cards. Use Lurrus to recur cheap permanents to maintain pressure. Don't overcommit; wait for them to blink or exhaust answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value Lurrus recursion and Thing in the Ice triggers. Prioritize keeping interaction in hand. Win by out-grinding with graveyard recursion and forcing them to overextend.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, increase removal and Fog while reducing counterspells. Against control/combo, add more targeted removal and counterspells. Always ensure your mana base can support the new card distribution.
notes: Thing in the Ice dies to Swords to Plowshares, so don't cast it into your own removal. Lurrus only recurs 2-mana or less permanents, so plan your graveyard accordingly. Watch out for Jewel Shops artifact hate or Oath of Druids combo turns. Avoid overcommitting to the board before turn 4.

philosophy: Oops! All Spells is a recursive spell-slinging archetype that aims to cast an overwhelming number of spells in a single turn to generate massive card advantage, mana acceleration, and board presence, ultimately closing out games with combo finishers. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that cannot handle explosive, multi-step turns, but struggles against fast, disruptive aggro and hard-counterspells.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (preferably fetch/shock/mana rocks) and at least one of Chrome Mox, Pact of Negation, or a spell-casting engine (e.g., Karn, Tamiyo, Raff). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early spells or mana acceleration, or hands with only one land and no acceleration. Keep any hand with Chrome Mox + a land, as it provides immediate tempo and card selection.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Chrome Mox or a cantrip (Brainstorm/Ponder) to dig for key pieces. Turn 2: Cast a mana rock or cheap spell, then follow up with a value engine or interaction. Prioritize fetching basic lands for shock lands and ensuring you have at least 3 colors of mana available by turn 3. Never tap out for a single spell if you can stabilize or dig for your combo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +1 Spell Pierce, +1 Force of Will, -2 Beseech the Mirror, -1 Smothering Tithe. vs Control: +2 Thassa's Oracle, +1 Kethis, +1 Narset, -2 Pact of Negation, -1 Force of Will. vs Combo: +1 Narset, +1 Force of Will, -2 Beseech, -1 Smothering. vs Mirror: +2 Thassa's Oracle, +1 Kethis, -2 Pact, -1 Force.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a secondary library. Use Karn/Tamiyo to recur cheap spells or protect key permanents. Never overextend into a known sweep unless you have a guaranteed win condition. Prioritize keeping 1-2 counterspells for protection, not just offense.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answered or bypassed their interaction. Hold back if they have unanswered removal or a hard counter. Use spells to trade efficiently (e.g., 1-for-1s) before going for the win.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for hand disruption (Thoughtseize, Liliana), sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger), and counterspells (Force, Negate). If they tap out for a single spell, they likely have a sweep or counter. If they keep mana open past turn 3, they're likely holding interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Beseech the Mirror for Thassa's Oracle or Kethis, the Hidden Hand. Griselbrand for card advantage to fuel the combo. Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath for mana and card draw.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 5+ mana available, a guaranteed Oracle/Kethis in hand or library, and no known hard interaction in their hand. Also commit if you can resolve a Griselbrand and draw into your win condition.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1 life with no spells in hand/library, or if they have a guaranteed hard counter/sweep in hand and you lack alternative win conditions. Also concede if you're facing a locked-out board with no recursion or interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig past counters, cast spells in response to their counters, or use Pact of Negation/Force of Will to protect your combo. Bolas's Citadel can bypass counters by drawing cards.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap spells, use discard to disrupt their mana base, and prioritize fast combo turns before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play defensively with counterspells and discard, stabilize with Griselbrand or Karn, and close out quickly once you have board control.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with spell recursion, use Narset to disrupt their draw steps, and go off with a massive spell count to overwhelm their interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Prioritize hand disruption, protect your combo pieces with counterspells, and win by resolving your Oracle/Kethis first. Use Narset to steal their key spells or disrupt their draw.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo deck to a more interactive, midrange-control hybrid. Bring in more counterspells and discard against control/aggressive decks, and lean into your combo finishers against slower decks. Always ensure your mana base can support 3 colors consistently.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to a single win condition without backup. Unique interaction: Beseech the Mirror can target itself to tutor for multiple copies of the same spell, enabling infinite recursion with certain engines. Always track opponent's hand size; if they have 0 cards, they're likely holding interaction.

philosophy: Stiflenought is a control-combo hybrid that stabilizes early with efficient interaction and card advantage, then closes out games by recurring Phyrexian Dreadnought via Lurrus of the Dream-Den while using Stifle to protect key permanents and disrupt opponent interaction. It wins by out-grinding opponents through graveyard recursion and out-resolving their removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 playable lands, Lurrus, Stock Up, or early interaction (Stifle, removal). Drop hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or hands consisting solely of removal without card draw or a finisher. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a spell on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cantrip or hold up interaction depending on matchup. Turn 2: Cast Lurrus or play a second land. Sequence lands to ensure blue/white mana for Stifle and Lurrus. If facing aggro, hold interaction for early threats; against control, establish card advantage with Stock Up or Lurrus.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Dredge, Oath, Initiative): Bring in additional removal, discard, and early interaction. Vs Control (Esper Lurrus, Jewel Shops): Bring in more counterspells, card advantage, and protection. Vs Combo: Bring in graveyard hate and specific disruption. Vs Mirror: Bring in extra recursion protection and interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a secondary hand; only cast Dreadnought when you have a plan to recur it or it applies immediate pressure. Use Stock Up to dig for answers or recursion pieces. Avoid overcommitting to the board without Lurrus or interaction ready.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction or recursion locked in. Dreadnought is a liability if unprotected or unrecurable. Use Stifle to protect Lurrus or Dreadnought from removal, and to disrupt opponent interaction when you are about to win.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Scavenging Ooze, etc.), counterspells, and sweepers. Note when opponents hold up mana for removal versus interaction. Track how many cards they have in hand to gauge if they can answer your next threat.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phyrexian Dreadnought cast from the graveyard and recurring via Lurrus, or a board presence of Lurrus plus Dreadnought. Sometimes a Stifle-protected Dreadnought that cannot be removed wins the game outright.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have graveyard recursion secured, enough mana to cast Dreadnought, and interaction to protect it. Also commit when the opponent has no removal or graveyard hate in play.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if graveyard hate is in play and you cannot remove it, if you are out of resources and the opponent has a sweeper, or if you are facing a degenerate combo you cannot disrupt. Also concede if you are dead to the board and have no way to rebuild.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Stifle to protect your own spells or Dreadnought. Cast threats in response to counterspells when possible. Use cantrips to dig around interaction and force them to spend mana on multiple threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to disrupt their mana development. Use Stifle to counter key ramp spells or mana dorks. Dreadnought can stabilize by applying pressure while they are still ramping.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Hold interaction for key threats and use Lurrus to recur removal. Dreadnought can be a clock if you survive the early turns. Use Stifle to protect your board or disrupt their combat tricks.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Stock Up and Lurrus recursion. Pressure with Dreadnought to force interaction and break their lock. Use Stifle to protect your win conditions from their removal or counterspells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Treat it as a resource war. Prioritize graveyard protection, out-value with card draw, and use Stifle to protect your Lurrus and Dreadnought from their recursion. Win by having more interaction and better graveyard management.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure control to matchup-specific hate. Bring in more graveyard hate vs. combo and aggro, more counterspells and protection vs. control, and extra recursion protection in the mirror. Adjust land counts if the format requires more or fewer basics, and ensure you have enough colored sources for Stifle and Lurrus.
notes: Common mistake: casting Dreadnought without a plan to recur it or protect it. Stifle timing is crucial—save it for opponent's removal or key spells, not just for fun. Lurrus is fragile; protect it or have a backup plan. In an Unknown format, adapt to the local meta but keep the core recursion engine intact. Always track opponent's graveyard and hand size to avoid walking into sweepers or hate cards.
